3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Trompeter, N., Singh, G., Ganson, K. T., Testa, A., Jackson, D. B., Assari, S., Murray, S. B., Bibbins-Domingo, K., & Baker, F. C. (2022). Social epidemiology of early adolescent cyberbullying in the United States. Academic Pediatrics, 22(8), 1287-1293.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We conducted a secondary cross-sectional analysis of data from the 2-year follow-up of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study (4.0 release)... After omitting study participants with missing data for cyberbullying or sociodemographic variables (Supplemental Appendix), 9429 children remained in the analytic sample.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Total daily screen time and screen time by modality (hours/day)
3C_Exposure.modalities: total screen time, TV shows/movies, videos (YouTube), video games (single-player), video games (multi-player), texting, video chat, social media, internet
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Screen use for the following modalities was determined using adolescents' self-reported hours of use on a typical weekday and weekend: multi-player gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing the internet, and watching/streaming movies, videos, or TV. Total typical daily screen use was calculated as the weighted sum ([weekday average x 5] + [weekend average x 2])/7.

3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Adolescents completed a self-reported questionnaire to capture cyberbullying (victimization and perpetration) based on the validated Cyberbullying Scale. Cyberbullying victimization was assessed with the question, 'Have you ever been cyberbullied, where someone was trying on purpose to harm you or be mean to you online, in texts, or group texts, or on social media (like Instagram or Snapchat)?'

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multiple logistic regression with propensity weights
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: sex, race/ethnicity, sexual orientation, country of birth, household income, parent education, study site
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Multiple logistic regression analyses were conducted to estimate cross-sectional associations between sociodemographic factors (both models included sex, race and ethnicity, sexual orientation, country of birth, household income, parents' highest education) as independent variables and lifetime cyberbullying victimization or perpetration as outcomes, controlling for study site. We additionally used multiple logistic regression analyses to estimate associations between screen time and lifetime cyberbullying victimization or perpetration in unadjusted and adjusted models.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Each additional hour of total screen time was associated with 1.11 (95% CI 1.08-1.14) higher odds cyberbullying victimization
1.10 (95% CI 1.06-1.14) higher odds of cyberbullying perpetration in adjusted models
Table 3: Internet - Cyberbullying Victimization Adjusted OR 1.28 (1.13-1.45), p<.001
Table 3: Social media - Cyberbullying Victimization Adjusted OR 1.21 (1.15-1.27), p<.001
Table 3: Internet - Cyberbullying Perpetration Adjusted OR 1.25 (1.09-1.43), p=0.001
Table 3: Social media - Cyberbullying Perpetration Adjusted OR 1.19 (1.12-1.27), p<.001

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: The effects of some sociodemographic factors were low. The potential for selection bias may be represented by a greater proportion of ethnic/racial minorities and parents with lower education excluded from the analysis. The strengths of this study are derived from the large, diverse, contemporary, and national sample.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper briefly notes 'effects were low' in limitations but does not constrain interpretation. Proceeds to issue extensive recommendations.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Pediatricians can consider assessing for cyberbullying and provide support and anticipatory guidance for early adolescents. The American Academy of Pediatrics advocates for a Family Media Use Plan, which could incorporate guidance on family discussions on cyberbullying.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.per_unit_verbatim: Each additional hour of total screen time was associated with 1.11 (95% CI 1.08-1.14) higher odds cyberbullying victimization and 1.10 (95% CI 1.06-1.14) higher odds of cyberbullying perpetration
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion focuses on describing cyberbullying prevalence rates and sociodemographic correlates. It notes internet and social media as the modalities most strongly associated with cyberbullying. The paper issues clinical recommendations for pediatricians to screen for cyberbullying and incorporate it into family media use plans, though the cross-sectional design limits causal interpretation.
